stem: Using the reduction formula for \( \int \sin^{n} x\,dx \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\sin^{[[n]]} x\,dx \).
solution: The Wallis reduction \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin^{n}x\,dx = \tfrac{n-1}{n}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin^{n-2}x\,dx \) applied down to \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin x\,dx = 1 \) or \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2}1\,dx = \tfrac{\pi}{2} \) gives, after multiplying by \( [[c]] \), the value \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the reduction one step too few, doubling the result.
This applies the reduction one step too many, halving the result.
This mishandles a factor in the recursion, tripling the result.
This mishandles a factor in the recursion, scaling by 1/3.
This uses the wrong \( \tfrac{n-1}{n} \) factor at the last step.
This uses the wrong \( \tfrac{n-1}{n} \) factor at the last step.

stem: Using the reduction formula \( \int \tan^{n} x\,dx = \dfrac{\tan^{n-1}x}{n-1} - \int \tan^{n-2}x\,dx \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/4} [[c]]\tan^{[[n]]} x\,dx \).
solution: The telescoping tangent reduction \( \int \tan^{n}x\,dx = \tfrac{\tan^{n-1}x}{n-1} - \int\tan^{n-2}x\,dx \), applied repeatedly down to \( \int_0^{\pi/4}\tan x\,dx = \tfrac12\ln 2 \) or \( \int_0^{\pi/4}1\,dx = \tfrac{\pi}{4} \), gives \( [[answer]] \) after scaling by \( [[c]] \) (note the sign alternates at each step, unlike the sine/cosine Wallis recursion).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly reduces all the way to the base case \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) without keeping the algebraic \( \tan^{n-1}/(n-1) \) terms picked up along the way.
This makes the same base-case error but with the wrong sign.
This adds an extra spurious \( [[c]] \) from a miscounted recursion step.
This subtracts an extra spurious \( [[c]] \) from a miscounted recursion step.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.

stem: Using the substitution \( u = \tan x \) (so \( \sec^{2}x\,dx = du \)) to reduce the even secant power, evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/4} [[c]]\sec^{[[n]]} x\,dx \).
solution: Write \( \sec^{[[n]]}x = (1+\tan^{2}x)^{[[n]]/2 - 1}\sec^{2}x \) and substitute \( u=\tan x \); the limits become \( u=0 \) to \( u=1 \), and expanding \( (1+u^{2})^{[[n]]/2-1} \) as a polynomial in \( u \) and integrating termwise gives, after scaling by \( [[c]] \), \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the polynomial expansion entirely and just reports the leading coefficient times \( [[c]] \).
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This adds a spurious extra term of \( [[c]] \) from a miscounted binomial expansion.
This subtracts a spurious extra term of \( [[c]] \) from a miscounted binomial expansion.
This scales the correct value by 3/2.

stem: Since the cosine power \( [[n]] \) here is odd, evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\sin^{[[m]]} x\cos^{[[n]]} x\,dx \) by peeling off one factor of \( \cos x \) and substituting \( u=\sin x \).
solution: With \( u=\sin x \), \( du=\cos x\,dx \), and \( \cos^{[[n]]-1}x=(1-u^{2})^{([[n]]-1)/2} \); the integral becomes \( [[c]]\int_{0}^{1}u^{[[m]]}(1-u^{2})^{([[n]]-1)/2}\,du \), a polynomial integral in \( u \) that evaluates to the rational number \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the naive Beta-function denominator \( m+n \) without the correct binomial expansion.
This uses the naive denominator \( m+n+2 \), off by the correct combinatorial factor.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This adds a spurious extra \( [[c]] \) from a miscounted expansion.
This wrongly reuses the pure-Wallis \( \pi/4 \) form, which only applies when both powers are even.

stem: Since BOTH powers here are even, the quick substitution trick fails; use the full double-angle Wallis reduction to evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\sin^{[[m]]} x\cos^{[[n]]} x\,dx \).
solution: Because both exponents are even, no factor of \( \sin x \) or \( \cos x \) can be peeled off for a clean substitution; repeatedly applying the double-angle Wallis reduction to both powers (or the general product formula involving the Beta function) yields a rational multiple of \( \pi \): \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses a naive denominator \( m+n \) for the \( \pi \) coefficient instead of the correct product-formula factors.
This uses a naive denominator \( m\cdot n \) for the \( \pi \) coefficient instead of the correct product-formula factors.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by a factor of 4 from the correct value.
This is off by a factor of 1/4 from the correct value.
